title: Beyond the Biotech Race — Right-Sizing U.S.-China Competition in a Human-Centered Industry
gist: ASPI working paper reframes U.S.-China biotech rivalry as 'asymmetric convergence' across a distributed value chain rather than a linear race: the U.S. leads in basic science, FDA credibility and capital while China now leads in research scale, clinical-trial speed, manufacturing and out-licensing. It argues against semiconductor-style blanket decoupling and for a layer-specific 'protect, promote, partner, shape' strategy plus narrow de-risked health cooperation.

insights.claim: U.S.-China biotech competition is better modeled as 'asymmetric convergence' across a multi-layered value chain (basic science, translational research, clinical trials, manufacturing, regulation, commercialization, data, healthcare delivery) rather than a single linear zero-sum race.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Investors should stop pricing biotech competition as a single chokepoint contest; each value-chain layer has different winners and different exposure to policy shocks.
insights.quote: It is asymmetric convergence across a distributed value chain, with each country gaining strength at different layers of the biotechnology ecosystem.
insights.locator: Asymmetric Convergence Across a Distributed Value Chain

insights.claim: Biotechnology has no single decisive chokepoint equivalent to advanced lithography in semiconductors, which makes export-control-style containment ineffective for slowing China's biotech progress.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Policy bets premised on chokepoint controls (BIS-style) are likely to fail in biotech; trades keyed to 'biotech decoupling' may misjudge the mechanism.
insights.quote: Biotechnology does not have a single decisive chokepoint equivalent to advanced lithography in semiconductors.
insights.locator: I. Introduction

insights.claim: China can challenge U.S. biotech leadership without replicating the full U.S. innovation model, remaining weaker in foundational discovery while leading in applied research, clinical execution, manufacturing scale and commercialization.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Watch downstream/operational layers (CDMO, trials, out-licensing) for Chinese share gains even while U.S. keeps the discovery crown.
insights.quote: China does not need to become the United States to challenge the United States.
insights.locator: III. Understanding Asymmetric Convergence

insights.claim: China's biotech capabilities are embedded institutional capacities built over years of learning and global integration, making them durable across policy cycles and unlikely to be reversed by any single tool or a fixed three-year competitive window.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Discount narratives of a closing 'window' that can be slammed shut; Chinese biotech share is a structural, multi-year trend.
insights.quote: Its progress may evolve unevenly across sectors, but it is unlikely to be reversed through policies framed around a fixed competitive window.
insights.locator: Why Convergence Is Durable

themes.theme: U.S. Structural Advantages and Their Fragility

insights.claim: U.S. biotech leadership rests on four renewable pillars—unrivaled basic-science/university research (NIH, NSF), first-in-class platform discovery, a deep life-sciences VC and capital-markets ecosystem, and global FDA regulatory credibility—none of which is self-sustaining.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: U.S. biotech moat is policy-dependent; funding/immigration/regulatory shocks directly threaten the discovery engine that underpins valuations.
insights.quote: the United States boasts an unparalleled basic science ecosystem and university research enterprise that constitute the crown jewel of the nation
insights.locator: The United States: Leadership through Renewal

insights.claim: Proposed NIH/NSF funding cuts, pressure on universities, FDA capacity uncertainty and restrictive immigration policy threaten to compound and gradually erode the linked U.S. funding-talent-discovery-startup-capital-FDA pipeline.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Sustained NIH/NSF budget cuts are a structural negative for the U.S. early-stage biotech pipeline; monitor appropriations as a sector signal.
insights.quote: If these links weaken simultaneously, the United States could gradually lose the strengths that have historically made it the global leader in biomedical innovation.
insights.locator: The United States: Leadership through Renewal

insights.claim: Internationally trained scientists, including Chinese-born researchers, are increasingly relocating to Europe or returning to China amid U.S. political and immigration uncertainty, simultaneously strengthening China's ecosystem and weakening a historic U.S. advantage.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Talent outflow is a leading indicator of long-run U.S. biotech erosion; watch return-migration and overseas hub formation.
insights.quote: more talent may relocate to Canada, Europe, Singapore, China, and other emerging innovation hubs
insights.locator: The United States: Leadership through Renewal

insights.claim: Per the 2025 Nature Index, eight of the world's ten leading research institutions are now Chinese, with Harvard the only U.S. institution remaining in the top ten.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: A stark marker of China's scientific scale shift; the geography of high-output research has rebalanced toward China.
insights.quote: eight of the world’s ten leading research institutions are now Chinese, with Harvard the only American institution remaining in the top ten
insights.locator: Science and Research Capacity

insights.claim: China's 2025 R&D spending reached roughly $569 billion and has surpassed the U.S. in total R&D on a purchasing-power-parity basis, with basic research crossing 7% of total R&D for the first time.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: China's sustained high-single-digit R&D growth signals a multi-decade compounding advantage in research capacity.
insights.quote: In 2025, China’s research spending reached approximately $569 billion
insights.locator: Science and Research Capacity

insights.claim: China's 2025 reforms cut innovative-drug review to 60 working days (30 for some qualifying drugs) via implied-approval systems, and investigator-initiated trials needing only hospital-level approval have accelerated early proof-of-concept development.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: China's regulatory speed structurally lowers drug-development time and cost, drawing global trial activity onshore.
insights.quote: the introduction of implied approval systems has reduced innovative drug review to 60 working days, and for some qualifying innovative drugs to 30 working days under 2025 reforms
insights.locator: Clinical Development, Commercialization, and Industrial Scale

insights.claim: Chinese firms now account for roughly 30% of experimental drugs in development worldwide and more than a quarter of global pharma licensing transactions, completing over 150 out-licensing deals worth more than $130 billion in 2025 alone.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Big Pharma pipelines are increasingly sourced from China; in-licensing of Chinese assets (ADCs, GLP-1, CAR-T) is now a core deal-flow channel to watch.
insights.quote: In 2025 alone, Chinese biotechnology companies completed more than 150 out-licensing deals with a combined value exceeding $130 billion.
insights.locator: Clinical Development, Commercialization, and Industrial Scale

insights.claim: China's co-located regional clusters such as Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park (Shanghai) and Suzhou BioBAY shorten feedback loops by placing labs, hospitals, regulators, firms and manufacturing in close proximity, compressing the drug-development cycle.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Cluster co-location is the mechanism behind China's out-licensing momentum; a model U.S. policymakers may copy via regional hubs.
insights.quote: Zhangjiang Hi-Tech Park in Shanghai and Suzhou BioBAY
insights.locator: Clinical Development, Commercialization, and Industrial Scale

insights.claim: China leads where biotech behaves as an industrial/engineering system (scaling output, fast trials, biologics manufacturing, cluster integration, asset generation) while the U.S. leads where it behaves as a discovery/translational ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: A clean map of comparative advantage: short Chinese-strength layers (manufacturing/trials) for U.S. reshoring upside, long them for execution speed.
insights.quote: China’s strengths are most pronounced where biotechnology functions as an industrial and engineering system
insights.locator: Asymmetric Convergence Across a Distributed Value Chain

insights.claim: China's publication volume should not be confused with scientific impact; its strength remains concentrated in engineering, optimization and scale-up rather than generating new paradigms, foundational discoveries or novel research tools.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: China's lead is operational, not yet frontier-creative; first-in-class novelty remains a defensible U.S. edge.
insights.quote: Publication volume, however, should not be confused with equivalent scientific impact.
insights.locator: Science and Research Capacity

insights.claim: China faces a relative scarcity of physician-scientists who bridge lab and clinic because hospital incentives prioritize patient throughput over research, leaving the bench-to-bedside translation layer a comparative U.S. strength.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Translational medicine and academic-medical-center integration remain durable U.S. moats even as China scales trials.
insights.quote: the relative scarcity of physician-scientists who can bridge the gap between laboratory research and clinical application
insights.locator: China’s Remaining Constraints

insights.claim: Whether AI ultimately favors the U.S. or China in biotech is unresolved: the U.S. leads in frontier models, compute and cloud, while China brings clinical data generation, trial execution and manufacturing scale, so integration across both fields will decide the outcome.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: AI-bio winners will be integrators, not single-domain leaders; watch firms combining frontier models with clinical data assets.
insights.quote: success will depend less on leadership in either field individually and more on the ability to integrate advances across both
insights.locator: AI and the Next Frontier

insights.claim: AI protein-design tools can generate modified DNA sequences for hazardous proteins that evade existing DNA-synthesis screening, and China receives more than 30% of global DNA synthesis orders, making bilateral screening standards a shared-interest safeguard.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: AI-bio misuse is a tail risk creating regulatory pressure on DNA-synthesis firms; screening standards are an emerging compliance burden.
insights.quote: China receives more than 30% of global DNA synthesis orders, effective safeguards require meaningful implementation on both sides
insights.locator: 5. Establish Risk-Reduction Dialogue on AI-Bio Convergence

insights.claim: Agentic AI systems that autonomously execute tasks and coordinate workflows could dramatically lower barriers to biological experimentation, amplifying the scale, speed and accessibility of potentially dangerous activities.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Agentic AI raises bio-misuse risk and invites access controls/oversight regimes on biological design tools.
insights.quote: agentic systems can autonomously execute tasks, use tools, coordinate workflows, and make iterative decisions with limited human supervision
insights.locator: 5. Establish Risk-Reduction Dialogue on AI-Bio Convergence

insights.claim: Supply chains and APIs are the one biotech layer where reducing China dependence is not self-defeating, because unlike rare earths APIs are not geographically fixed and domestic fine-chemistry capacity can be rebuilt via subsidies, procurement guarantees and regulatory support.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: API reshoring is the most viable component of biotech strategy; watch procurement-guarantee and subsidy programs for domestic API/fine-chem plays.
insights.quote: There is no element of API manufacturing and production of biologics that physically is controlled by China and could not be reshored or diversified.
insights.locator: Layer Four: Supply Chains, APIs, and Industrial Resilience

insights.claim: WuXi, the dominant contract manufacturing organization, illustrates that China's biotech advantage comes from deep integration across the drug-development stack, not control of any single chokepoint, making it resistant to semiconductor-style export-control logic.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: BIOSECURE-style restrictions on WuXi address dependence but cannot replicate semiconductor chokepoint leverage; integration is the moat.
insights.quote: China’s advantages in biotech stem not from control over any single technological chokepoint but from deep integration across the drug development stack
insights.locator: Targeted Protection Rather Than Broad Disengagement

insights.claim: India is an indispensable diversified-resilience partner for generics, APIs and vaccines, but is not a clean China substitute because many upstream starting materials for India's API output are themselves sourced from China.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: India reshoring narratives overstate independence; the China-upstream linkage caps the de-risking benefit of an India pivot.
insights.quote: many upstream starting materials that go into India’s API output are sourced from China
insights.locator: India as a Strategic Partner

themes.theme: Policy Strategy: Protect, Promote, Partner, Shape

insights.claim: Effective U.S. biotech policy cannot be merely 'anti-China' and defensive but must be 'pro-America', running faster by reinvigorating NIH/NSF funding, streamlining FDA pathways, de-risking private capital, and building regional biomanufacturing hubs.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: The investable policy thesis is domestic capacity-building (FDA reform, biomanufacturing, capital catalysts), not restriction.
insights.quote: effective policy to compete in biotech cannot be merely “anti-China” and defensive but rather must be “pro-America”
insights.locator: The Limits of Containment

insights.claim: Blanket decoupling or BIOSECURE-style restrictions on Chinese biomanufacturing service providers, applied without viable U.S. alternatives, could raise costs and disrupt development timelines for U.S. biotech firms in the short term.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: BIOSECURE passage is a near-term cost/timeline headwind for U.S. biotechs dependent on Chinese CDMOs like WuXi.
insights.quote: reducing reliance on Chinese biomanufacturing service providers may address real security concerns
insights.locator: The Limits of Containment

insights.claim: Pending bills (BINSA, COINS Act, BIOSECURE) reflect concern over U.S.-China biopharma licensing, but defensive measures alone do not strengthen U.S. innovation drivers and are most effective when paired with affirmative capacity-building.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Legislative-risk watch list for biopharma: BINSA, COINS, BIOSECURE could curb China licensing deal flow.
insights.quote: Recent congressional proposals, including the Biotechnology Innovation and National Security Act (BINSA), the COINS Act, and related legislation
insights.locator: Recent Legislative Responses

insights.claim: An effective U.S. approach should be organized around four objectives—Protect (security-sensitive data, supply chains, dual-use tech), Promote (domestic capacity), Partner (narrow bounded cooperation), and Shape (global standard-setting).
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: A clean policy taxonomy for parsing which biotech measures help versus harm U.S. competitiveness.
insights.quote: an effective approach should be organized around four complementary objectives: protecting, promoting, partnering, and shaping
insights.locator: A Four-Part Strategy: Protect, Promote, Partner, and Shape

insights.claim: The Biden administration's January 2025 controls on high-parameter flow cytometers and certain mass spectrometry equipment show genuine dual-use concern but also why biotech cannot be managed through semiconductor-style chokepoint controls.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Equipment export controls are narrow and won't move the broad biotech needle; concrete precedent for the chokepoint critique.
insights.quote: The Biden administration’s January 2025 controls on high-parameter flow cytometers and certain mass spectrometry equipment
insights.locator: The Limits of Containment

themes.theme: Health as a Stabilizer and De-Risked Cooperation

insights.claim: Health and medicine remain one of the few viable stabilizers in the U.S.-China relationship because shared disease burdens, asymmetric capabilities and the humanitarian nature of biomedicine make narrow cooperation possible where few other sectors allow it.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Health is the most likely bilateral de-escalation channel; cooperation signals here may presage broader thaw.
insights.quote: health and medicine remain a rare domain characterized by shared vulnerabilities and overlapping national interests
insights.locator: V. Beyond Competition: Why Health Is Different

insights.claim: Trump-Xi summit readouts listed 'health' immediately after 'economy and trade' on the Chinese side, likely intentional signaling that Beijing views health as one of few areas where cooperation with Washington remains possible.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Diplomatic tell that health cooperation has top-level Chinese backing; a concrete catalyst to monitor.
insights.quote: The Chinese side explicitly listed “health” immediately after “economy and trade” as an area for expanded cooperation—an ordering likely intentional.
insights.locator: V. Beyond Competition: Why Health Is Different

insights.claim: Greater international standardization of clinical-trial and regulatory requirements could enable more concurrent oncology approvals, potentially reducing global cancer mortality by 10-20% and saving one to two million lives annually (per a Harvard Business Review analysis of pembrolizumab and enzalutamide delays).
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Quantifies the upside of trial-data harmonization; oncology is the highest-impact cooperation pilot.
insights.quote: potentially reducing global cancer mortality by 10–20% and saving on the order of one to two million lives annually
insights.locator: 1. Build Disease-Specific Clinical Data Corridors, Starting with Cancer

insights.claim: China's large population makes ultra-rare diseases like thalassemia (carrier rates up to 14.13% for alpha- and 4.91% for beta-thalassemia in some southern regions) a complementary cohort resource U.S. patients cannot assemble domestically.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Rare-disease trial recruitment is a concrete, low-security cooperation niche; China's National GeneBank is a strategic biobank asset.
insights.quote: carrier rates for α-thalassemia and β-thalassemia are as high as 14.13% and 4.91%, respectively, in some southern regions of China
insights.locator: 2. Create Rare Disease Partnerships Where Chinese Patient Cohorts Can Help U.S. Patients

insights.claim: More than $200 billion in annual pharmaceutical revenue faces loss of exclusivity through 2030 (including major GLP-1 therapies), pressuring Big Pharma to cut costs while sustaining pipelines and creating incentive to leverage China's cost advantages.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: The 2030 patent cliff is a structural earnings risk pushing pharma toward Chinese R&D/manufacturing despite dependency concerns.
insights.quote: More than $200 billion in annual pharmaceutical revenue is estimated to face loss of exclusivity through 2030
insights.locator: 6. Explore Opportunities to Improve Affordability without Creating Dependency

insights.claim: A purely restrictive U.S. posture risks accelerating a China-led global biopharma development ecosystem operating outside U.S. influence, potentially forcing American cancer patients to travel abroad to access China-developed drugs unavailable at home.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: The strategic downside of decoupling is U.S. marginalization in global drug development; European acceptance of China data is the leading indicator.
insights.quote: American cancer patients traveling to China or Europe to access lifesaving China-developed drugs unavailable at home
insights.locator: VII. Conclusion

insights.claim: China is moving upstream toward inquiry-driven science via new institutions like Westlake University modeled partly on leading global universities, signaling ambition beyond incremental innovation.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Watch China's new research universities as a leading indicator of a future move into frontier discovery.
insights.quote: Institutions such as Westlake University reflect efforts to create research environments modeled in part on leading global universities.
insights.locator: III. Understanding Asymmetric Convergence

insights.claim: China modernized its IP/regulatory framework via the 2021 Patent Law (patent linkage and term extensions resembling U.S. Hatch-Waxman) and new drug-trial data-exclusivity provisions, creating a more innovation-oriented legal environment.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: China's IP reform reduces a historic friction for domestic innovators and foreign partners, supporting its asset-formation momentum.
insights.quote: The 2021 Patent Law introduced patent linkage mechanisms and patent term extensions that bear similarities to provisions in the U.S. Hatch-Waxman framework.
insights.locator: Regulatory and Legal Adaptation

insights.claim: China combines state-backed capital (government guidance funds) with private investment in a hybrid model that crowds in private capital and supports longer investment horizons, a partial rebalancing toward a U.S.-style market model.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Government-guided funds are the financing engine behind Chinese company formation; a model the paper urges the U.S. to partly adopt.
insights.quote: Innovations such as government guidance funds complement earlier industrial policy approaches
insights.locator: Capital Formation and Industrial Policy

insights.claim: American policymakers argue China's data approach is fundamentally non-reciprocal—benefiting from foreign data and global networks while restricting access to its own human genetic resources and patient data.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Data non-reciprocity is the core friction blocking deeper cooperation; HGR-regime reform is the unlock to watch.
insights.quote: China’s approach remains fundamentally non-reciprocal—benefiting from access to foreign data and global scientific networks while maintaining substantial restrictions on access to its own data resources
insights.locator: Data and the AI-Biology Nexus
